items.instructions: Choose the missing Kyrgyz lines to finish the greeting and name exchange.
successCriteria: Ask for a name with the polite pattern.
items.completionHint: If the speakers do not know each other yet, stay polite.

supportCards.title: Polite `сиз` forms are the safest first choice
supportCards.body: When you meet someone for the first time, polite address is the beginner-safe default. This task keeps you inside that safer register.

supportCards.title: Say `Саламатсызбы` as one greeting block
supportCards.body: Do not pause in the middle. It works better as one memorized respectful greeting chunk than as a word you sound out syllable by syllable every time.

supportCards.title: Respectful openings do real social work
supportCards.body: In many settings across Kyrgyzstan, a respectful opening formula can matter as much as the information that follows. A careful greeting changes the tone of the exchange immediately.

items.objective: Read key greeting lines in Cyrillic and match them to meaning.
items.instructions: Read each Kyrgyz line first. Use transliteration only when needed.
successCriteria: Recognize the polite greeting in Cyrillic.
items.completionHint: Read the Cyrillic first, then check the transliteration if you get stuck.

supportCards.title: Question shape matters as much as vocabulary
supportCards.body: `Сиздин атыңыз ким?` is a reusable name-question pattern, not just three isolated words. Learn the whole frame and you can swap in new content later.

supportCards.title: Let the final question rise slightly
supportCards.body: A light question lift on `ким?` helps the line sound like a real name question instead of a flat word list.

supportCards.title: Names usually arrive early in Kyrgyz social small talk
supportCards.body: A short exchange often moves to names quickly, so mastering this question gives the learner an early real-world payoff.

items.objective: Introduce yourself in a respectful first meeting.
items.instructions: Imagine you are meeting a host family for the first time. Greet them, say your name, and close warmly.
successCriteria: End with a polite closing or thanks.
items.completionHint: Use the phrase bank, then swap in your own name.

supportCards.title: `Менин атым ...` is a safe low-risk self-introduction
supportCards.body: This frame avoids extra grammar load. You can introduce yourself clearly before you learn more flexible identity structures.

supportCards.title: Pause after the greeting, not inside the name frame
supportCards.body: A natural beginner rhythm is `Саламатсызбы | Менин атым ...`. Keep `Менин атым` together so it sounds like one introduction unit.

supportCards.title: Host-family style first meetings are a good beginner frame
supportCards.body: Many early Kyrgyz interactions for learners happen in homes, classrooms, or hosted settings. Practicing respectful self-introductions in that frame pays off quickly.

items.scenario: You are being introduced to a new host family in Bishkek.
